coredata.dc:description: Due to the lack of resistant germplasm, sclerotinia stem rot (SSR) caused by S. sclerotiorum (Sclerotinia sclerotiorum) has become the most destructive disease in rapeseed (Brassica napus L.) production. Nanobiotechnology has recently been considered as a sustainable approach for crop protection by reducing pesticide use. In this study, a novel blue-green fluorescent nanocomplex derived from licorice and wolfberry (LW-NCs) was synthesized and characterized, exhibiting stronger antioxidant activity compared with licorice or wolfberry extracts alone. LW-NCs promoted the growth of rapeseed seedlings without affecting photosynthesis efficiency. In vitro antifungal assays revealed that LW-NCs significantly inhibited the growth of S. sclerotiorum at a concentration of 200 ppm. Transcriptomic and metabolomic analyses demonstrated that foliar application of LW-NCs increased the JA content and the activities of SOD and CAT in rapeseed, ultimately enhancing the tolerance of rapeseed to S. sclerotiorum. In addition, toxicity experiments have demonstrated that LW-NCs possess excellent biocompatibility. These results indicated that the application of LW-NCs can not only improve the disease resistance of rapeseed, but also help reduce the harm of pesticides to human health, ensuring the safety of them for agricultural applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The characterization of LW-NCs. (A) The illustration for preparation of LW-NCs. (B) The TEM size of LW-NCs. (C) The zeta potentional of LW-NCs. (D) Hydrodynamic diameter of LW-NCs. (E) The TEM image of LW-NCs. (F) Fluorescence spectrum of LW-NCs. (G-I) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, O1s, and N1s in LW-NCs. Confocal images of LW-NCs in a rapeseed leaf (J) and fluorescent intensity of LW-NCs in rapeseed leaf (K). Statistical analyses were performed using a Student's t test: *P < 0.05.

The effect of 200 ppm root applications of LW-NCs on rapeseed seedling development under normal growth conditions. Confocal images of LW-NCs in a rapeseed root (A) and fluorescent intensity of LW-NCs in rapeseed root (B). (C) The growth phenotype of rapeseed seedling. Scale bars = 2 cm. (D) The length of hypocotyl at different time points. (E) The phenotypic performance of rapeseed seedlings and the second true leaf treated with buffer and LW-NCs. Scale bars = 2 cm. (F) The area of leaves. (G) The fresh weight of leaves. (H) The fresh weight of roots. (I) Total chlorophyll content of leaves. (J) The Chlorophyll content index of leaves. Statistical analyses were performed using a Student's t test: ns, Not significant; *P < 0.05; * *P < 0.01; * **P < 0.001. The values are the means ± s.d. at least three biological replicates. Each dot represents individual data points.

Foliar application of 200 ppm LW-NCs significantly improve rapeseed tolerance to S. sclerotiorum. (A-D) LW-NCs inhibits the growth of S. sclerotiorum in vitro. (A) Mycelium morphology of S. sclerotiorum on PDA agar plates without (CK) and with LW-NCs treatment, respectively. Images of S. sclerotiorum cultured on PDA plates at 23℃ for 2 days in the dark. Scale bars, 100 μm. Each assay was replicated three times. (B) Antifungal efficacy of LW-NCs against S. sclerotiorum. The values are the means ± s.d. All of the experiments were repeated at least three times with similar results. (C) The sclerotium formation of S. sclerotiorum cultured on PDA plates. (D) The number of microsclerotia. (E-G) LW-NCs inhibits the growth of S. sclerotiorum in vivo. Disease symptom area was photographed (E) and measured (G) at 36 h post-inoculation (hpi). Scale bars = 2 cm. (F) NBT staining (for superoxide anion) and DAB staining (for hydrogen peroxide) of leaves from S. sclerotiorum-inoculated rapeseeds treated with buffer (CK) and LW-NCs. Scale bars= 1 cm. (H-J) LW-NCs reduced H2O2 content and increased superoxide dismutase (SOD) and catalase (CAT) activities in the LW-NCs treated plants. (H) The H2O2 content. (I) The SOD activity. (J) The CAT activity. In H-J, the values are the means ± s.d. at least three biological replicates. Each dot represents individual data points. Statistical analyses were performed using a Student's t test: ns, Not significant; *P < 0.05; * *P < 0.01; * **P < 0.001.

Increased JA content in LW-NCs treated plants. (A) Principal component analysis of DEGs at different time points in each group. (B) Venn diagram showing the number of DEGs in different transcriptomes. The 0h-up and 0h-down represent the number of upregulated and downregulated genes before inoculation. The 48h-up and 48h-down represent the number of upregulated and downregulated genes after 48 hours of inoculation, respectively. (C) Gene ontology enrichment analysis of differentially expressed genes upregulated and downregulated at different time points after inoculation, respectively. Gene ratio is the number of DEGs divided by the total number of genes associated with a specific pathway. (D) Heat map of the expression of genes involved in JA biosynthesis pathway after LW-NCs treatment. The gene expression value was calculated using the FPKM method. Upregulated and downregulated genes are shown in red and blue, respectively. (E) Measurement of JA content in the control and LW-NCs treated rapeseed plants. (F-H) qRT-PCR analysis of JA (LOX2, AOS1, JAR1) related genes in the control and LW-NCs treated rapeseed plants at different time points after inoculation. The values represent means ± SD, n = 3. β-actin (FJ529167.1) was used as the internal control gene. Statistical analyses were performed using a Student's t test: *P < 0.05; * *P < 0.01; * **P < 0.001.
A model for environmental friendly nanomaterials LW-NCs improve rapeseed growth and inhibit the infection of S. sclerotiorum in vitro and vivo, respectively.

head.ce:title: Licorice-wolfberry derived nanomaterials enhance sclerotinia stem rot resistance by activating JA-mediated immune response in rapeseed

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the lack of resistant germplasm, sclerotinia stem rot (SSR) caused by S. sclerotiorum (Sclerotinia sclerotiorum) has become the most destructive disease in rapeseed (Brassica napus L.) production. Nanobiotechnology has recently been considered as a sustainable approach for crop protection by reducing pesticide use. In this study, a novel blue-green fluorescent nanocomplex derived from licorice and wolfberry (LW-NCs) was synthesized and characterized, exhibiting stronger antioxidant activity compared with licorice or wolfberry extracts alone. LW-NCs promoted the growth of rapeseed seedlings without affecting photosynthesis efficiency. In vitro antifungal assays revealed that LW-NCs significantly inhibited the growth of S. sclerotiorum at a concentration of 200 ppm. Transcriptomic and metabolomic analyses demonstrated that foliar application of LW-NCs increased the JA content and the activities of SOD and CAT in rapeseed, ultimately enhancing the tolerance of rapeseed to S. sclerotiorum. In addition, toxicity experiments have demonstrated that LW-NCs possess excellent biocompatibility. These results indicated that the application of LW-NCs can not only improve the disease resistance of rapeseed, but also help reduce the harm of pesticides to human health, ensuring the safety of them for agricultural applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapeseed is one of the most extensively cultivated oilseed crops globally, with applications across various industrial and agricultural fields (Tileuberdi et al., 2022). However, SSR caused by S. sclerotiorum is a highly devastating disease of rapeseed worldwide, causing significant yield losses annually (Boland and Hall, 1994; Shang et al., 2024). Due to the limited availability of resistant germplasm, SSR is primarily managed through chemical pesticides. Extensive pesticide use may encounter challenges due to pathogen resistance and environmental considerations. The potential toxicity of pesticides may lead to acute poisoning or long-term health effects for both pesticide applicators and consumers of crops with pesticide residues (Jacobs et al., 2024; Silva et al., 2022). Hence, there is an urgent need to develop new techniques to prevent and control the occurrence of S. sclerotiorum to guarantee food security.

S. sclerotiorum is a necrotrophic phytopathogenic fungus that can produce numerous lytic enzymes, cell wall-degrading enzymes, oxalic acid and effectors, that breakdown the cell wall and induce necrosis (Albert et al., 2022; Rollins and Dickman, 2001). Plant tissue necrosis is usually caused by an outbreak of reactive oxygen species (ROS) at the site of infection. For example, during invasion, S. sclerotiorum induces the expression of plant defense genes and the synthesis of ROS by secreting the elicitor cerato-platanin protein (CP) (Yang et al., 2017, 2009). However, the overaccumulation of ROS causes lipid peroxidation, protein denaturation, and DNA injury, which ultimately results in oxidative stress (Kwak et al., 2006; Mittler et al., 2022). Such as citrus Huanglongbing (Ma et al., 2022) and Verticillium wilt of cotton (Qiu et al., 2023b). Therefore, the regulation of ROS homeostasis may be an effective way to alleviate plant disease damage.

Nanotechnology, including disease resistance, nutrient utilization, and crop yield, is considered the fifth technological revolution (Ahmed et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2016). For example, previous studies have shown that some nanomaterials with ROS scavenging properties, such as CeO2 (Liu et al., 2021a), ZnO@ZIF-8 (Liang et al., 2022) and PEI-MQDs (Qiu et al., 2023b), can be used to maintain the ROS balance within plant cells to enhance plant stress tolerance. Agricultural nanomaterials, including nanopesticides and nanocarriers, are mostly synthesized from industrial chemicals, which results in longer periods and higher costs for agricultural nanomaterial promotion and transformation (Asmatulu et al., 2022). For example, zinc oxide (ZnO), the most commonly used nanoparticles (Qiu et al., 2023a), might cause toxicity to human health, such as the respiratory system and other organs (Singh, 2019), which also suggests that low-toxicity green nanomaterials used to improve plant growth and yield could have significant advantages. For instance, carbon dots sourced from Salvia miltiorrhiza, which act as both Ca2 + signaling amplifiers and ROS scavengers, enhanced the environmental adaptability of plants (Li et al., 2022). But there are few reports of the use of herbal-derived nanomaterials, especially composite nanocomposites, for plant disease resistance.

It was observed that nanocomposites derived from herbal sources exhibit low toxicity, high water solubility, distinct luminescent characteristics, chemical stability, improved thermal resistance, and ease of functionalization (Luo et al., 2021). Moreover, the bioactive components inherent in plants have shown outstanding therapeutic effects on various diseases such as hemostasis, hypoglycemia, anti-infection, inflammation-related disorders, and tumors (Zeng et al., 2023). For example, nanomaterials derived from licorice and wolfberry have been reported to have multiple benefits, including antiaging, antioxidant, antidiabetic, anticancer, cell protection, antibacterial, neuroprotective, radiation-induced damage alleviation, cellular aging, and immune-regulatory effects (Guo et al., 2023; Miranda et al., 2024). These properties may make plant-based low-toxicity green nanomaterials not only harmless to humans and the environment while improving crop growth and yield, but they might even be beneficial if encountered.

Given the demonstrated antioxidant and bioactive properties of licorice and wolfberry, a novel licorice-wolfberry herbal-derived nanocomposite (LW-NCs) with a diameter of 11.76 nm and bule-green fluorescence emission were synthesized through a one-pot hydrothermal method, and techniques including transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were employed to characterize the LW-NCs properties. Soaking germinated rapeseed seeds in LW-NC solution promoted root development and seedling growth. Additionally, foliar application of LW-NCs significantly inhibited the occurrence of SSR in rapeseed by activating JA-dependent disease resistance. Furthermore, the activities of SOD and CAT in rapeseed plants were enhanced by LW-NCs, and the antioxidant system activity was boosted to mitigate pathogen-induced oxidative stress. The toxicity experiment results of LW-NCs in vitro and in vivo, further suggested their suitability as safe and environmentally friendly nanomaterials to ensure the safety of rapeseed throughout cultivation and consumption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plant material, pathogen cultivation, infection and disease assay
ce:section.ce:para: Rapeseed seedlings (Zhongshuang 11, a widely cultivated variety) were watered with Hoagland solution for 4 weeks in a greenhouse. The experiments were conducted at 23°C under long-day conditions with a 16-hour light/8-hour dark and a relative humidity of 70 %. Seedlings were sprayed with 200 ppm LW-NCs or buffer solution. Place the seedlings in dark for 3 hours and perform treatments. The relative chlorophyll content was analyzed using a chlorophyll meter (SPAD-502 plus, Konica Minolta, Japan). The phenotype and biomass of rapeseed were recorded.

For S. sclerotiorum (ATCC 18683) inoculation, the S. sclerotiorum was inoculated on a potato dextrose agar medium and placed in an incubator at 23°C for 3 days. Rapeseed leaves were inoculated with 7 mm diameter agar plugs and placed in plates lined with moist filter paper to maintain humidity (Lin et al., 2022). Lesion areas were measured using ImageJ. Leaf samples were collected at different time points post-inoculation and stored at −80°C. Each treatment had three biological replicates, with 15 plants per replicate.

Dissolve 0.5 g of DAB (3,3′-diaminobenzidine) in 450 mL of distilled water, adjusting the pH to 3.8 with 0.1 M HCl. In a separate container, dissolve 1 g of NBT (nitro blue tetrazolium) in 50 mL of 50 mM sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.5). Immerse the rapeseed leaves in the staining solution for 8 hours in the dark at room temperature. After staining, wash the leaves with deionized water to remove the staining solution. To extract chlorophyll, boil the leaves in a mixture of ethanol and glycerol (9:1, v/v).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of herbal-based nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Two hundred mg of licorice or wolfberry powder was added to an autoclave containing 10 mL of deionized water, and the mixture was heated at 160°C for 6 hours. After heating, the solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 1 hour to collect the supernatant, resulting in licorice-based mesoporous spheres (Li-MSs) or wolfberry-based carbon dots (Wo-CDs). For the synthesis of the LW-NCs, 100 mg of each licorice and wolfberry powder was combined in an autoclave with 10 mL of deionized water, and the solution was heated at 160°C for 6 hours. The final solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 1 hour to obtain the supernatant, which contained the LW-NCs. The synthesized nanomaterials were stored at 4°C for future use.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained using a FEI Tecnai F20 microscope (Hillsboro, Oregon) operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Samples for TEM were prepared by applying the solution dropwise onto a copper mesh. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses were performed with a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (ThermoFisher, USA) and a Bruker D8 Advance 25 (Bruker, Germany), respectively, at Shiyanjia Lab in Wuhan, China. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was conducted using an Avatar-330 spectrometer (ThermoFisher, USA). The nanoparticles' size and zeta potential were characterized with a Malvern Zetasizer Nano 90 (Malvern, U.K.), while photoluminescence spectra were recorded using an LS55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Company, USA).

The H₂O₂ scavenging rate was calculated by monitoring the decrease in absorbance at 240 nm using a microplate spectrophotometer. In this procedure, Li-MSs were added to a 2 mM H₂O₂ solution at a concentration of 200 ppm, and the reaction rate was measured over 5 minutes at 25 °C. The H₂O₂ concentration was determined using a molar extinction coefficient of 39.4 M/cm.The hydroxyl radical (•OH) scavenging rate was assessed following previously established methods (Shan et al., 2023). In brief, a 200 μL mixture containing Tris-HCl buffer (0.1 M, pH 4.7), methyl violet (24 μM), FeSO₄ (1.08 mM), H₂O₂ (0.1 M), and Li-MSs (with a final nanoparticle concentration of 200 ppm) was incubated at 37 °C for 30 minutes. For the blank control, a separate 200 μL mixture containing only Tris-HCl buffer (0.1 M, pH 4.7) and methyl violet (24 μM) was used. The scavenging rate was calculated as follows:
Scavenge rate= (1-δAS/δAB) × 100%
δAS=AC-AS
δAB=AC-AB
AC is the absorbance of standard sample; AS is sample absorbance; AB is the absorbance of blank control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS assay and enzyme activity assay
ce:section.ce:para: The DCF (2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate, indicating H2O2) fluorescent dye was employed to observe ROS in rapeseed leaves (Wu et al., 2018). Leaf discs (5 mm in diameter) were incubated in the dark with 25 μM DCF dye for 30 minutes in TES infiltration buffer (pH 7.5). Following incubation, the leaf discs were rinsed three times with deionized water and placed onto a glass slide for imaging. The samples were examined using a Leica SP8 confocal microscope (Leica Microsystems, Germany), and the fluorescence intensity of DCF was analyzed using LAS (Leica Application Suite) AF Lite software.

The quantification of hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂), superoxide dismutase (SOD), and catalase (CAT) were measured using the test kit for H₂O₂, SOD, and CAT from Suzhou Grace Biotechnology Co., Ltd, following the manufacturer instructions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The distribution of LW-NCs in root cells
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the distribution of LW-NCs in rapeseed root cells, the roots were incubated in a solution containing LW-NCs (200 mg/L) or in water as a control, with the roots fully submerged in the dark for 3 hours. The distribution of LW-NCs in the roots was observed using a Leica SP8 confocal microscope (Leica Microsystems, Germany). Imaging was performed with the following settings: PMT1 (550–610 nm) for LW-NCs fluorescence; and PMT2 (700–750 nm) for chloroplast fluorescence. Colocalization analysis was conducted using the LAS (Leica Application Suite) AF Lite software.

RNA extraction from rapeseed and S. sclerotiorum was performed according to the protocols provided in the plant RNA Extraction Kit (Tiangen Biotech, Beijing, China) and the fungus RNA extraction kit (OMEGA, R6840–02), respectively. First-strand cDNA was synthesized using SuperScript III reverse transcriptase (Invitrogen, Carlsbad, CA, USA). Quantitative reverse transcription PCR (qRT-PCR) was conducted using a 7500 Real-Time PCR system (ABI, Foster City, CA, USA) in a 15 μL reaction volume. The qRT-PCR protocol included an initial denaturation step at 95°C for 3 minutes, followed by 40 cycles of 95°C for 15 seconds and 60°C for 45 seconds. Three biological replicates were performed for each treatment. Relative gene expression levels were calculated using the 2−ΔΔCt method, with β-actin (FJ529167.1) and β-tubulin serving as internal controls for rapeseed and S. sclerotiorum, respectively. The primers used in this study are listed in Supplementary Table 1.

Transcriptome analysis was performed on rapeseed leaves treated with either buffer or LW-NCs. Samples were collected both before inoculation (0 hpi) and 48 hours (48 hpi) post-inoculation. RNA was isolated from the leaves of rapeseed plants infected with S. sclerotiorum. Each treatment having three biological replicates. Library preparations were sequenced on the Illumina NovaSeq 6000 platform, generating 150-bp paired-end reads. High-quality RNA-Seq reads were aligned to the Zhongshuang 11 genome. To identify DEGs, the following thresholds were applied: FPKM > 1, fold change ≥ 2, and P < 0.05. The P < 0.05 threshold was also used to identify significantly enriched Gene Ontology (GO) terms.

Samples of rapeseed leaves treated with either buffer or LW-NCs were collected at various time points post-inoculation. The leaves were quickly ground into a powder using liquid nitrogen, and 0.1 g of the powdered sample was placed into a 2 mL centrifuge tube. The sample was homogenized in 700 µL of 80 % (v/v) methanol and shaken overnight at 4°C in the dark. Afterward, the samples were centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 minutes at 4°C, and the supernatant was collected. Next, 300 µL of precooled extraction buffer was added to the precipitate, followed by shaking and extracting at 4°C for 1 hour. The samples were centrifuged again at 12000 rpm for 15 minutes at 4°C, and the resulting solution was filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane at 4°C. Plant hormones were measured using HPLC-MS/MS (Qiu et al., 2023c).

Mouse macrophage RAW264.7 cells were cultured in high-glucose Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM; Hyclone), supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS; Gibco), in a humidified atmosphere containing 5 % CO₂ at 37°C. The toxicity of LW-NCs to macrophages was assessed using the Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) (Beyotime, China). RAW 264.7 cells were seeded into 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 10⁵ cells per well and incubated overnight to allow for cell adhesion. After adhesion, various concentrations of LW-NCs (500, 250, 100, 50, 25, and 0 ppm) were added, and the cells were incubated for 24, 48, and 72 hours. Following the incubation period, 10 μL of CCK-8 dye and 90 μL of fresh serum-free medium were added to each well and incubated for 1 hour at 37°C. The absorbance was then measured at 450 nm using a microplate reader (Biotech, Synergy Limited). Each experiment was repeated three times to ensure reproducibility.

Fresh whole blood (5 mL) was collected from healthy human veins and centrifuged at 3500 rpm for 15 minutes at 4°C. The supernatant was discarded, and the washed red blood cells were resuspended in physiological saline to prepare a 4 % red blood cell suspension. Various concentrations of LW-NCs were added to the 4 % red blood cell suspension at concentrations of 500, 250, 100, 50, 25, and 0 ppm. Physiological saline served as the negative control, while ddH₂O and 0.5 % Triton X-100 were used as positive controls. After gentle vortexing, the samples were incubated in a constant temperature water bath at 37°C for 2 hours. Following the incubation, each sample was centrifuged at 3500 rpm for 5 minutes at 4 °C, and the supernatant was collected. A volume of 100 μL of the supernatant was transferred to a 96-well plate, and the absorbance at 450 nm was measured using a microplate reader. Each experiment was repeated three times for reliability. The hemolysis rate was calculated using the following formula: Hemolysis Rate (%)= (OD_experiment group - OD_negative control) / (OD_positive control - OD_negative control) × 100 % (0.5 % TritonX-100 was used for the calculation of the positive control group).

In this study, male C57BL/6 mice (n = 10; age 6 weeks) were utilized. These mice were obtained from the Hubei Provincial Center for Disease Control and Prevention and were housed in a pathogen-free SPF animal laboratory at the School of Stomatology, Wuhan University. They were kept in a controlled environment with a 12-hour light/dark cycle and maintained at a temperature of 22°C ± 2°C. For one week prior to the experiments, the mice were provided with a standard pelleted diet and sterilized water. All animal experimentation was approved by the Ethics Committee of The Hospital of Stomatology, Wuhan University (Approval number: S07923060K) and followed the ARRIVE (Animal Research: Reporting of In vivo Experiments) guidelines.

Mice were randomly assigned to two groups (n = 5 per group): (1) the LW-NCs exposure group, which received a concentration of 500 ppm in a volume of 200 μl every two days; (2) The control group, which was administered the same volume of sterile deionized water. All mice were orally gavaged every two days for a duration of 2 weeks. To minimize reflux reactions, solutions were delivered directly into the lower esophagus after a 2-hour fasting period. The body weight of each animal was recorded every two days throughout the study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo assessment of multi-organ toxicity
ce:section.ce:para: The organs (liver, spleen, lung, kidney, and heart) of the mice were harvested and fixed in 4 % paraformaldehyde for 24 hours before being embedded in paraffin. After fixation, the tissue sections were rehydrated through a series of graded ethanol solutions (100 %, 95 %, 80 %, and 70 %) to restore their water content. Following rehydration, the tissues were sliced into 5 μm sections for histological staining. Hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining was then performed on the sections, and photomicrographs of the stained samples were captured using an optical microscope.

Herbal based nanomaterials (LW-NCs) with an average diameter of 11.76 nm were synthesized by one pot hydrothermal treatment of licorice and wolfberry powder at 160°C for 6 hours ( Fig. 1A, B). The DLS results indicate that the zeta potential and diameter of LW-NCs are −22.85 eV and 14.86 nm, respectively (Fig. 1C-D). The TEM image of LW-NCs shows its spherical particles and good monodispersity (Fig. 1E). High resolution transmission electron microscopy shows that LW-NC is a sphere with doping points, smaller than Li-MSs and Wo-CDs (Fig. 1E, S1 A-B). The nanomaterials has different fluorescence emission wavelengths (Li-MS is 490–640 nm, LW-NC is 510–610 nm, Wo-CD is 550–870 nm) (Fig. 1F and S1C, D). The synthesized herbal based nanomaterials are mesoporous spheres, dots, and spheres. As shown in Fig. 1F, upon excitation at 483 nm, the LW-NCs exhibited a maximum emission peak at 562 nm. There are no obvious peaks in the XRD results (Fig. S2A). The chemical structure of LW-NCs was further characterized by XPS and FTIR spectroscopy. Figure S2B shows the absorption bands at 400–4000 cm−1 for 1633.41, 2102.99, 3250.43, and 3335.7 cm−1. XPS investigation spectra show that LW-NCs were composed of C, O, and N, with atomic percentages of 44.5 %, 5.4 %, and 50.1 %, respectively (Fig. S2C). The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum shows three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, and 288.0 eV, attributed to C-C, C-O-C, and O-CO bonds, respectively (Fig. 1G). The high-resolution O1s XPS spectrum shows two peaks at 531.8 and 532.7 eV, attributed to the C-O and CO bands, respectively (Fig. 1H). The high-resolution N1s XPS spectrum shows two peaks at 399.9 eV and 400.9 eV, respectively, attributed to C-NH2 and satellite jitter (Fig. 1I).

To verify that LW-NCs can enter rapeseed leaf cells, confocal laser microscopy is used to observe the distribution of LW-NCs in the leaves of rapeseed. Compared with those of the control, the leaves treated with LW-CNs exhibited more obvious fluorescence and greater fluorescence intensity (Fig. 1J-K). The H2O2 and OH• scavenging efficiencies of the 200 ppm LW-NCs were 33.18 % and 51.35 %, respectively, which were significantly greater than those of 200 ppm Li-MSs and Wo-CDs (Fig. S3 A,B). UV stress is thought to induce ROS accumulation in plant leaves. To demonstrate the ability of LW-NCs to scavenge ROS in vivo, we observed the fluorescence of ROS in rapeseed leaves after UV stress with the fluorescent dye DCF (2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate, indicating H2O2). After 1 h of UV stress, compared with that in the control group, the fluorescence of DCF in the LW-NCs treatment group was weak (Fig. S4A), and the fluorescence intensity was significantly reduced (Fig. S4B). These results indicate that LW-NCs have good ROS scavenging ability both in vivo and in vitro.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LW-NCs promote the growth of rapeseed seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: Research found that nanoparticles can stimulate plant growth by affecting seed germination, root growth or grain yield. To investigate the impact of LW-NCs on rapeseed seedlings growth, five-day-old rapeseed plants with consistent root lengths were selected, and the roots were exposed to 200 ppm LW-NCs for 3 h before being transplanted into nutrient solution for growth. As shown in Fig. 2A, a clear fluorescence signal was observed in rapeseed root after the roots were exposed to LW-NCs. In the control, almost no fluorescence signal was observed, indicating that LW-NCs successfully entered the root cells (Fig. 2A, B). After 6 days, the roots of the plants treated with LW-NCs were significantly longer than those of the control plants (Fig. 2C, D). Three weeks later, the harvested leaf area, biomass, and physiological parameters were analyzed. Compared with those in the untreated control group, the leaf area, leaf fresh weight and root fresh weight significantly increased in the LW-NCs-soaked group but did not affect the chlorophyll content or chlorophyll content index of the leaves (Fig. 2E-J). However, Rapeseed seedlings treated with 200 ppm Wo-CDs or Li-MSs showed no significant difference in root length or fresh weight compared to the untreated control group (Fig. S5). Overall, the findings indicate that LW-NCs have remarkable biocompatibility and can serve as effective fertilizers for promoting crop growth.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LW-NCs improve rapeseed tolerance to S. sclerotiorum
ce:section.ce:para: Rapeseed leaves exhibited no symptoms at 0–12 hpi but developed necrosis at 24–48 hpi following inoculation with S. sclerotiorum (Fig. S6A). Staining of rapeseed leaves revealed increased ROS levels upon invasion by S. sclerotiorum (Fig. S6A). Analysis of necrotic areas at various time points corresponded with the observed plant disease phenotype (Fig. S6B). However, excessive ROS accumulation can not only accelerate plant cell death but also facilitate the colonization of necrotrophic fungal pathogens. Therefore, LW-NCs have been applied to maintain ROS balance in infected rapeseed leaves. First, in vitro fungal experiments showed that the growth rate of S. sclerotiorum was lower on PDA agar plates 2 days after exposure to 200 ppm LW-NCs compared with the control ( Fig. 3A, B). There was no significant difference in the mycelial morphology of S. sclerotiorum (Fig. 3A), while the number of microsclerotia decreased (Fig. 3C-D). The expression of genes related to micronucleus development (SsPka1) and mycelial growth (SsFKH1) were also inhibited in S. sclerotiorum treated with LW-NCs (Fig. S7 A,B). However, Wo-CDs and Li-MSs do not affect the growth rate of the mycelium of S. sclerotiorum (Fig. S8). To further clarify whether pre-treatment of rapeseed with nanomaterials can inhibit S. sclerotiorum colonization, rapeseed leaf infection assays were conducted. The results showed that foliar application of 200 ppm LW-NCs significantly suppressed SSR damage in rapeseed according to measurements of the disease symptom area at 36 hpi (Fig. 3E-G), while there was no significant difference in the resistance of rapeseed leaves sprayed with Wo-CDs and Li-MSs alone to S. sclerotiorum compared to the control group (Fig. S9). Consistent with these findings, the NBT and DAB histochemical staining experiments showed similar results. Fewer blue and brown spots were detected in the leaves of the LW-NC-treated rapeseed plants inoculated with S. sclerotiorum than in those of the buffer control plants (Fig. 3F), indicating that in the later stage after inoculation, the rapeseed leaves sprayed with the LW-NCs maintained ROS homeostasis. To further confirm this finding, the H2O2 content was assessed using an H2O2 content assay kit. The H2O2 levels in rapeseed leaves treated with LW-NCs were significantly lower compared with those in leaves from the buffer control group (Fig. 3H). Furthermore, increased activities of superoxide dismutase (SOD) and catalase (CAT) were observed in LW-NCs-treated leaves at 36 hpi compared with control leaves (Fig. 3I, J), indicating enhanced enzyme activities due to LW-NCs treatment. These findings suggested that LW-NCs can mitigate the excessive accumulation of ROS in plants by activating antioxidant systems such as SOD and CAT, thereby enhancing rapeseed tolerance to S. sclerotiorum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LW-NCs stimulate the JA-mediated immune response in rapeseed
ce:section.ce:para: To expand the understanding of the molecular mechanism underlying how LW-NCs enhance the tolerance of rapeseed to S. sclerotiorum, transcriptome analysis was conducted on rapeseed plants treated with or without LW-NCs before and after inoculation. First, principal component analysis (PCA) revealed that both the LW-NCs treatment group and the control group were clearly separated before and after inoculation ( Fig. 4A), suggesting that rapeseed induced transcriptional reprogramming after exposure to LW-NCs. Before inoculation (0 hpi), more DEGs were upregulated in rapeseed sprayed with LW-NCs for 3 h (Fig. 4B). However, more DEGs were inhibited at 48 hpi, with 485 out of 1583 DEGs upregulated and 1098 downregulated (Fig. 4B). Furthermore, to understand the functional significance of these changes, the DEGs were mapped to terms in the Gene Ontology (GO) database. As shown in Fig. 4C, at 0 hpi, the upregulated DEGs were mainly enriched in jasmonic acid (JA) metabolic process, sesquiterpenoid metabolic process, response to insect, phenylpropanoid metabolic process and abscisic acid metabolic process, etc. The downregulated DEGs were mainly enriched in response to iron (II) ion, response to ethanol, response to nickel cation, obsolete auxin homeostasis and mannan catabolic process, etc. Furthermore, GO enrichment analysis at 48 hpi revealed that the upregulated DEGs were significantly enriched in response to hydrogen peroxide, benzene-containing compound metabolic process, regulation of innate immune response, regulation of response to biotic stimulus and plant-type hypersensitive response, etc. (Fig. 4C). The downregulated DEGs were enriched in indole-containing compound biosynthetic process, tryptophan metabolic process, aromatic amino acid metabolic process, indoleacetic acid biosynthetic process and defense response by callose deposition in cell wall, etc. (Fig. 4C). These findings demonstrated that foliar application of LW-NCs activates JA-related defense responses before inoculation in rapeseed leaves, and during the late stage of pathogen infection, the expression of defense response genes remains induced in the leaves of rapeseed plants treated with LW-NCs.

Based on the transcriptome data, it was found that key genes involved in the JA biosynthesis pathway were upregulated after LW-NCs treatment, suggesting that JA may be involved in LW-NC-induced basal resistance in rapeseed (Fig. 4D). To further confirm this, metabolome analysis of rapeseed leaves treated with or without LW-NCs before and after inoculation was conducted. The results showed that LWNCs treatment increased the accumulation of JA levels in rapeseed leaves, as measured by LCMS/MS (Fig. 4E). Consistent with the results of the metabolomic analyses, the expression levels of key genes related to JA signaling were determined at different stages of S. sclerotiorum infection via qRTPCR analyses. The gene related to JA-related genes (including JAR1, LOX2, AOS1) were greater than that in the control, with JAR1 and AOS1 having a greater expression level in the early stage and LOX2 having a greater expression level in the late stage of pathogen invasion (Fig. 4F-H). In addition, the expression of the gene related to PTI (PGIP1) was also highly expressed in plants sprayed with LW-NCs from 0 to 48 hpi, with a peak at 6 hpi (Fig. S10). Overall, these results indicated that LW-NCs may trigger JA-mediated defense networks, inducing metabolic and transcriptional reprogramming and ultimately conferring enhanced pathogen resistance to rapeseed seedlings.

With the rapid development of nanotechnology, there is an urgent need for a thorough investigation and risk assessment of the toxicity of nanomaterials on human health. Therefore, a detailed evaluation of its toxicity was conducted in vitro and in vivo. First, mouse monocyte macrophages were cultured with LW-NCs solution, and a CCK-8 assay was performed to assess cytotoxicity to mouse immune cells. Fig. S11A showed that LW-NCs had no obvious cellular toxicity effect on RAW 264.7 cell line. Additionally, at concentrations ranging from 0.5 to 500 ppm, the cell activity increased in a concentration-dependent manner at 48 h and 72 h (Fig. S11B). Additionally, as expected, the LW-NCs exhibited no significant hemolytic activity when co-cultured with healthy human blood cells (Fig. S11C and D). The highest hemolysis rate was calculated to be only 0.893 % (< 5 %), indicating excellent safety of the LW-NCs for humans (Fig. S11E). To determine whether the LW-NCs are safe for swallowing, a 14-day feeding mouse model was carried out, as shown in Fig. S11F. After two weeks, there were no significant differences in the body weight changes of the mice in LW-NCs group (Fig. S11G). And the HE stainings of heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney also showed no obvious organ toxicity of LW-NCs in C57BL/6 mice (Fig. S11H). These results demonstrated the minimal toxicity and good degradability of LW-NCs in body, making them highly suitable for agricultural applications especially in edible crops.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis and functional properties of herbal-based nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials produced from industrial raw materials are known to contribute to water and soil pollution (Khan et al., 2021). Agricultural nanomaterials derived from Chinese medicine offer a sustainable alternative. Prior studies have demonstrated that nanomaterials synthesized from a single Chinese medicine can enhance plant salt tolerance (Li et al., 2022), yet research on utilizing agricultural nanomaterials from Chinese medicine to support plant disease resistance remains limited. This study is the first to apply herbal-derived composite nanocomposites to plant disease resistance. By comparing the fluorescence spectra of three types of nanomaterials, it was found that there were significant differences between LW NCs, Wo-CDs, and Li-MSs, indicating that their electronic structures had undergone changes (Fig. 1, S1). Meanwhile, compared to Wo-CDs and Li-MSs, LW-NCs exhibited superior ROS scavenging efficiency and antifungal activity (Figs. S3, S8). The chemical properties and electronic structure of nanomaterials, especially catalysts, are significantly related (Xu et al., 2021). Multicomponent combined nanomaterials, such as heterojunction (Li et al., 2021) and single-atom doped materials (li et al., 2024), may have more unique catalytic properties. Traditional Chinese medicine is often applied in combination with multiple components, and the resulting composite structure may have a different structure than the individual components (Zhang et al., 2015). In this study, the three materials consist of dots, mesoporous spheres, and smaller mesoporous spheres loaded with carbon, in comparison to Li-MSs (Fig. 1, S1). The reduced diameter of LW-NCs may be influenced by components in wolfberry during the hydrothermal synthesis process. A smaller diameter enhances the likelihood of entering plant cells to exert effects (Zhang et al., 2015), while a delivery system incorporating Wo-CDs loaded onto Li-MSs is often more efficient than using CDs alone (Zhang et al., 2015).

While progress has been achieved in utilizing nanomaterials for crop disease prevention and control, the focus has primarily been on enhancing crop disease resistance phenotypes through nanomaterial applications (Adisa et al., 2018; Yan et al., 2023). For example, Ahmed et al. (Ahmed et al., 2022) reported that bioengineering chitosan-iron nanocomposites (BNCs) can manage bacterial leaf blight disease in rice by modulating plant defense responses and improving nutritional uptake. Similarly, Shang et al.(Shang et al., 2020) found that foliar exposure to copper sulfide nanoparticles reduces bakanae disease incidence in rice through mechanisms involving contact-mortality, nutritional modulation, and phytohormone regulation. However, the potential mechanisms through which nanomaterials enhance crop disease resistance remain largely unexplored.

Plant hormones, as cellular signaling molecules, play pivotal roles in regulating signaling networks involved in plant responses to biotic and abiotic stresses (Pieterse et al., 2012). Previous studies have shown that the salicylic acid (SA) and jasmonic acid (JA) defense pathways are usually antagonistic, and that SA is involved in activating defense responses against biotrophic and hemi-biotrophic pathogens (Grant and Lamb, 2006). In contrast, JA is usually associated with defending against necrotrophic pathogens (Bari and Jones, 2009). In this study, LW-NCs enhance the activities of SOD and CAT to mitigate excessive ROS accumulation and suppress cell death (Fig. 3). Furthermore, LW-NCs significantly up-regulated the expression of PGIP1, LOX2, AOS1 and JAR1, which are involved in PTI and JA synthesis in rapeseed shoots compared with the control (Fig. 4, S10). These findings suggest that LW-NCs not only exhibit in vitro antifungal activity but also stimulate a JA-mediated immune response in plants when applied via foliar spraying, providing a new strategy for crop protection ( Fig. 5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Safety of herbal-based nanomaterials in agriculture
ce:section.ce:para: While pesticide use is crucial for enhancing agricultural yields, concerns persist regarding their adverse effects on human health (Munir et al., 2024). Chronic consumption of food from crops frequently sprayed with high pesticide doses can lead to acute and chronic illnesses. Although acute pesticide poisoning rates have declined, subclinical long-term effects remain a concern (Kalyabina et al., 2021). Efforts to mitigate pesticide residues in food include exploring novel techniques. With the expanding use of nanomaterials, researchers are investigating their potential toxicity alongside their beneficial effects. Current studies on the biological safety of herbal-derived carbon dots have primarily focused on in vitro cytotoxicity and hemolysis experiments. The toxicity of carbon dots in cellular interactions may be influenced by factors such as dosage, light activation, particle size, positive surface charge, and local charge density (Sun et al., 2023). Studies on carbon dots derived from licorice and wolfberry have shown that they enhance cell activity within a concentration range of 19.5–5000 ppm without inducing significant toxicity (Liu et al., 2021b). Similarly, carbon dots from Lycium barbarum have been found to promote bone marrow stem cell proliferation in a dose-dependent manner at concentrations ranging from 50 to 600 ppm (Guo et al., 2023). Similar to the results of this study, LW-NCs at concentrations of 25–500 ppm exhibited no significant toxicity to monocyte-derived macrophages and even promoted cell proliferation. Hemolysis experiments confirmed their compatibility with blood components, indicating their safety in vivo. Long-term administration at high doses did not result in notable organ changes in mice, underscoring their favorable metabolic profile in vivo (Fig. S11). This study represents the first comprehensive safety analysis of nanomaterials in both plant and animal applications. These results suggested that LW-NCs, even when present in crops and consumed by humans, pose no significant health risks, ensuring the safe application of these nanomaterials in food production.

In summary, this study demonstrates that herbal-based nanomaterials LW-NCs synthesized from licorice and wolfberry, significantly promote plant growth. The LW-NCs exhibit excellent ROS scavenging capabilities and antifungal activity, which contribute to maintaining ROS homeostasis in infected plants and enhance the disease resistance of rapeseed against S. sclerotiorum. Moreover, transcriptomic and metabolomic analyses reveal that LW-NCs stimulate JA-mediated defense responses. Importantly, toxicity assessments indicate that LW-NCs are safe for agricultural use, making them a promising sustainable alternative to conventional pesticides (Fig. 5). This research highlights the potential of utilizing herbal-derived nanomaterials in enhancing crop resistance and supporting sustainable agricultural practices.

All procedures followed were in accordance with the ethical standards of the responsible committee on human experimentation (institutional and national) and with the Helsinki Declaration of 1975, as revised in 2008 (5). Informed consent was obtained from all patients for being included in the study.
